title: Advanced Model Evaluation & Diagnostics
description: Beyond accuracy: model calibration, confidence intervals, statistical model comparison, regression diagnostics, and learning curves for production ML.
details: Proper model evaluation separates production-grade ML from academic prototyping. This file covers model calibration (reliability diagrams, Platt scaling, isotonic regression, temperature scaling), confidence intervals for metrics (bootstrapping, normal approximation), statistical tests for comparing models (McNemar, Wilcoxon, 5x2cv paired t-test), ROC analysis pitfalls (partial AUC, ROC convex hull), regression diagnostics (residual analysis, Q-Q plots, heteroscedasticity, multicollinearity), and learning/validation curves for diagnosing bias and variance. These techniques prevent embarrassing production failures and are essential for Staff+ ML interviews.

sections.title: Model Calibration
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: A well-calibrated model's predicted probabilities match reality — among samples with predicted probability 0.8, roughly 80% should be positive. Many models (especially neural nets and boosted trees) produce overconfident probabilities.

Calibration methods:

  • Reliability Diagram: Bin predictions by probability (0-0.1, 0.1-0.2, ...). For each bin, plot average predicted probability vs actual fraction of positives. The diagonal is perfect calibration. A curve below the diagonal = overconfident; above = underconfident.
  • Expected Calibration Error (ECE): Weighted average of |actual - predicted| across bins. ECE < 0.05 is good. ECE > 0.1 needs calibration.
  • Platt Scaling: Train a logistic regression on model logits (pre-sigmoid outputs) to calibrate probabilities. Uses the validation set. Simple, effective, well-supported.
  • Isotonic Regression: Non-parametric calibration — fits a monotonic step function to map uncalibrated scores to calibrated probabilities. More flexible than Platt, but can overfit on small validation sets.
  • Temperature Scaling: Divide logits by a scalar T > 0 before softmax. T = 1 is original. T > 1 softens probabilities (less confident). T < 1 sharpens them. Simple, preserves accuracy (only changes confidence, not predictions).
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.calibration import CalibratedClassifierCV, calibration_curve
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# === Reliability Diagram ===
prob_true, prob_pred = calibration_curve(
    y_test, y_pred_proba, n_bins=10, strategy='uniform'
)

# Plot
plt.plot(prob_pred, prob_true, 'o-', label='Model')
plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], '--', color='gray', label='Perfect')
plt.xlabel('Mean predicted probability')
plt.ylabel('Fraction of positives')
plt.legend()
plt.show()

# === Expected Calibration Error ===
ece = np.sum(np.abs(prob_true - prob_pred) * 
             np.histogram(y_pred_proba, bins=10)[0]) / len(y_test)
print(f'ECE: {ece:.3f}')

# === Platt Scaling (sklearn) ===
# Calibrate any classifier using Platt (method='sigmoid')
calibrated = CalibratedClassifierCV(
    model, method='sigmoid', cv=5
)
calibrated.fit(X_train, y_train)
calibrated_probs = calibrated.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

# === Isotonic Regression ===
calibrated_iso = CalibratedClassifierCV(
    model, method='isotonic', cv=5
)
calibrated_iso.fit(X_train, y_train)

# === Temperature Scaling (PyTorch) ===
# import torch
# import torch.nn as nn
#
# class TemperatureScaling(nn.Module):
#     def __init__(self):
#         super().__init__()
#         self.temperature = nn.Parameter(torch.ones(1))
#
#     def forward(self, logits):
#         return logits / self.temperature
#
# # Optimize T on validation set using NLL loss
# T_scaler = TemperatureScaling()
# optimizer = torch.optim.LBFGS([T_scaler.temperature], lr=0.01)

# === Compare calibration ===
print(f'Before: ECE={ece:.3f}')
_, prob_true_cal, prob_pred_cal = calibration_curve(
    y_test, calibrated_probs, n_bins=10, strategy='uniform'
)
ece_cal = np.sum(np.abs(prob_true_cal - prob_pred_cal) * 
                 np.histogram(calibrated_probs, bins=10)[0]) / len(y_test)
print(f'After Platt: ECE={ece_cal:.3f}')

sections.title: Confidence Intervals for Metrics
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: A single accuracy number hides uncertainty. If accuracy is 92%, is the true accuracy 91-93% or 85-99%? Confidence intervals tell you how reliable your metric estimate is.

Methods:

  • Normal Approximation: For large test sets, accuracy follows a normal distribution. 95% CI = μ ± 1.96 · σ where σ = √(μ(1-μ)/n). Works for accuracy, precision, recall. Fails for small n or metrics near 0 or 1.
  • Bootstrap: Resample test set with replacement B times (B=1000). Compute metric on each resample. 95% CI = [2.5th percentile, 97.5th percentile] of the B metric values. Works for ANY metric — accuracy, AUC, F1, correlation. The gold standard.
  • Percentile Bootstrap for AUC: AUC is a complex statistic. Bootstrap is the recommended approach. Resample test cases, recompute AUC, get percentiles.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, roc_auc_score
import numpy as np

# === Normal Approximation (for accuracy) ===
y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
acc = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
n = len(y_test)

# 95% CI for accuracy
z = 1.96
se = np.sqrt(acc * (1 - acc) / n)
ci_low, ci_high = acc - z * se, acc + z * se
print(f'Accuracy: {acc:.3f} (95% CI: [{ci_low:.3f}, {ci_high:.3f}])')

# === Bootstrap CI (any metric) ===
def bootstrap_ci(y_true, y_pred, metric_fn, n_bootstraps=1000, ci=95):
    n = len(y_true)
    indices = np.arange(n)
    scores = []
    
    for _ in range(n_bootstraps):
        boot_idx = np.random.choice(indices, size=n, replace=True)
        score = metric_fn(y_true[boot_idx], y_pred[boot_idx])
        scores.append(score)
    
    lower = np.percentile(scores, (100 - ci) / 2)
    upper = np.percentile(scores, 100 - (100 - ci) / 2)
    return lower, upper

# === Bootstrap for AUC ===
y_pred_proba = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

ci_low, ci_high = bootstrap_ci(
    y_test, y_pred_proba,
    lambda y, p: roc_auc_score(y, p),
    n_bootstraps=2000
)
print(f'AUC: {roc_auc_score(y_test, y_pred_proba):.3f} (95% CI: [{ci_low:.3f}, {ci_high:.3f}])')

# === Visualizing bootstrap distribution ===
# scores = [...]
# plt.hist(scores, bins=30)
# plt.axvline(ci_low, color='r', linestyle='--')
# plt.axvline(ci_high, color='r', linestyle='--')
# plt.show()

# === When n is small, bootstrap is essential ===
# For n=100, a single accuracy of 90% has huge uncertainty
# Bootstrap reveals the true range (maybe 80-96%)

sections.title: Statistical Tests for Model Comparison
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: When model A gets 92% and model B gets 91.5%, is B really worse, or is the difference just random noise? Statistical tests tell you if the improvement is significant.

Key tests:

  • McNemar's Test: For paired binary predictions. Construct 2x2 contingency table: how many samples (A correct, B wrong) vs (A wrong, B correct). Tests if the off-diagonal counts are significantly different. Chi-squared statistic: χ² = (b - c)² / (b + c). p < 0.05 = significant difference. Best for comparing two classifiers on the same test set.
  • Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test: For paired metric scores (e.g., accuracy from 5-fold CV). Non-parametric, doesn't assume normality. Ranks the differences between pairs. Better than paired t-test when differences aren't normally distributed.
  • 5x2cv Paired t-Test: Run 5 iterations of 2-fold CV. In each iteration, compute difference in performance between models A and B on each fold. Average the 10 differences. Compute t-statistic with 5 degrees of freedom. Recommended by Dietterich (1998) as the most reliable test for classifier comparison.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, train_test_split
from scipy.stats import wilcoxon
import numpy as np

# === McNemar's Test (paired predictions) ===
def mcnemar_test(y_true, pred_a, pred_b):
    """
    Returns chi-squared statistic and p-value.
    H0: no difference between A and B
    """
    from scipy.stats import chi2
    
    # Contingency table
    b = np.sum((pred_a == y_true) & (pred_b != y_true))  # A right, B wrong
    c = np.sum((pred_a != y_true) & (pred_b == y_true))  # A wrong, B right
    
    chi2_stat = (b - c) ** 2 / (b + c + 1e-10)
    p_value = 1 - chi2.cdf(chi2_stat, df=1)
    return chi2_stat, p_value

# Usage
chi2, p = mcnemar_test(y_test, pred_model_a, pred_model_b)
print(f'McNemar: chi2={chi2:.2f}, p={p:.4f}')

# === Wilcoxon Signed-Rank (paired CV scores) ===
def wilcoxon_model_comparison(model_a, model_b, X, y, cv=5):
    scores_a = cross_val_score(model_a, X, y, cv=cv, scoring='accuracy')
    scores_b = cross_val_score(model_b, X, y, cv=cv, scoring='accuracy')
    
    stat, p = wilcoxon(scores_a, scores_b)
    return scores_a.mean(), scores_b.mean(), stat, p

mean_a, mean_b, w_stat, p = wilcoxon_model_comparison(
    model_a, model_b, X, y, cv=10
)
print(f'Model A: {mean_a:.3f}, Model B: {mean_b:.3f}')
print(f'Wilcoxon: p={p:.4f} ({"significant" if p < 0.05 else "not significant"})')

# === 5x2cv Paired t-Test ===
def five_by_two_cv(model_a, model_b, X, y, n_iterations=5):
    from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold
    
    differences = []
    for i in range(n_iterations):
        # 2-fold CV
        cv = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=2, shuffle=True, random_state=i)
        for train_idx, test_idx in cv.split(X, y):
            X_tr, X_te = X[train_idx], X[test_idx]
            y_tr, y_te = y[train_idx], y[test_idx]
            
            model_a.fit(X_tr, y_tr)
            model_b.fit(X_tr, y_tr)
            
            acc_a = np.mean(model_a.predict(X_te) == y_te)
            acc_b = np.mean(model_b.predict(X_te) == y_te)
            differences.append(acc_a - acc_b)
    
    # Compute t-statistic
    mu = np.mean(differences)
    sigma = np.std(differences, ddof=1)
    t_stat = mu / (sigma / np.sqrt(len(differences)))
    
    from scipy.stats import t
    p_value = 2 * (1 - t.cdf(abs(t_stat), df=n_iterations - 1))
    return t_stat, p_value

sections.title: Regression Diagnostics
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: A high R² doesn't mean your regression model is correct. Residuals (errors = y - ŷ) must satisfy key assumptions. Violations mean your model is unreliable.

Key diagnostics:

  • Residual vs Fitted Plot: Plot residuals vs predicted values. Should be randomly scattered around zero. Patterns indicate problems: fan shape = heteroscedasticity (variance changes with prediction level). Curve = nonlinearity (model missed a pattern).
  • Q-Q Plot (Quantile-Quantile): Plot standardized residuals against normal quantiles. Points should follow the diagonal line. Deviations at the tails = heavy-tailed residuals (more extreme errors than normal distribution predicts).
  • Heteroscedasticity Tests: Breusch-Pagan test (null: homoscedastic = constant variance). p < 0.05 = heteroscedastic. Goldfeld-Quandt test for comparing variance across two subgroups.
  • Multicollinearity (VIF): Variance Inflation Factor measures how much a feature's variance is inflated due to correlation with other features. VIF > 5 (or > 10, depending on strictness) = problematic multicollinearity. For linear models, this inflates coefficient standard errors and makes interpretation unreliable.
  • Outlier Detection: Standardized residuals > 3 indicate potential outliers. Cook's Distance measures influence of each point on the model — high Cook's D = point dramatically changes coefficients when removed.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
import statsmodels.api as sm

# === Residual plot ===
y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
residuals = y_test - y_pred

plt.scatter(y_pred, residuals, alpha=0.5)
plt.axhline(0, color='r', linestyle='--')
plt.xlabel('Predicted')
plt.ylabel('Residuals')
plt.show()

# === Q-Q Plot ===
from scipy import stats
sm.qqplot(residuals, line='45')
plt.show()

# === Breusch-Pagan Test for heteroscedasticity ===
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import het_breuschpagan

# Using statsmodels OLS for full diagnostics
X_sm = sm.add_constant(X_train)
model_sm = sm.OLS(y_train, X_sm).fit()

bp_test = het_breuschpagan(model_sm.resid, X_sm)
print(f'Breusch-Pagan: LM={bp_test[0]:.2f}, p={bp_test[1]:.4f}')
# p < 0.05 = heteroscedasticity detected

# === VIF (Variance Inflation Factor) ===
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor

vif_data = pd.DataFrame()
vif_data['feature'] = feature_names
vif_data['VIF'] = [
    variance_inflation_factor(X_train, i) 
    for i in range(X_train.shape[1])
]
print(vif_data.sort_values('VIF', ascending=False))

# === Cook's Distance ===
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import OLSInfluence

influence = OLSInfluence(model_sm)
cooks = influence.cooks_distance[0]
influential_points = np.where(cooks > 4/len(y_train))[0]
print(f'Influential points (Cook's D > 4/n): {len(influential_points)}')

# === Durbin-Watson (autocorrelation in residuals) ===
from statsmodels.stats.stattools import durbin_watson

dw = durbin_watson(model_sm.resid)
print(f'Durbin-Watson: {dw:.3f}')
# DW ≈ 2: no autocorrelation
# DW < 1: positive autocorrelation (bad for time series)
# DW > 3: negative autocorrelation

sections.title: Learning Curves & Validation Curves
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Learning curves show train/validation error as a function of training set size. They diagnose bias vs variance and tell you whether adding more data will help.

Reading learning curves:

  • High bias (underfitting): Both train and validation error are high and converge early. Adding more data won't help — the model lacks capacity. Fix: more features, deeper model, reduce regularization.
  • High variance (overfitting): Train error is low, validation error is high, and they don't converge. The gap = variance. Adding more data WILL help — the curves will slowly converge.
  • Ideal fit: Train and validation error converge to the same low value. The gap is small and both are low.

Validation curves: Plot train/validation error as a function of a hyperparameter (e.g., max_depth, C, learning_rate). Find the parameter value where validation error is minimized — that's the sweet spot between underfitting and overfitting.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.model_selection import learning_curve, validation_curve
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# === Learning Curve ===
train_sizes, train_scores, val_scores = learning_curve(
    model, X, y,
    train_sizes=np.linspace(0.1, 1.0, 10),
    cv=5,
    scoring='neg_mean_squared_error',
    n_jobs=-1
)

train_mean = -np.mean(train_scores, axis=1)
train_std = np.std(train_scores, axis=1)
val_mean = -np.mean(val_scores, axis=1)
val_std = np.std(val_scores, axis=1)

plt.plot(train_sizes, train_mean, 'o-', label='Train')
plt.fill_between(train_sizes, train_mean - train_std, train_mean + train_std, alpha=0.1)
plt.plot(train_sizes, val_mean, 'o-', label='Validation')
plt.fill_between(train_sizes, val_mean - val_std, val_mean + val_std, alpha=0.1)
plt.xlabel('Training set size')
plt.ylabel('Error')
plt.legend()
plt.show()

# === Validation Curve (e.g., max_depth) ===
param_range = [1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 20]
train_scores, val_scores = validation_curve(
    model, X, y,
    param_name='max_depth',
    param_range=param_range,
    cv=5,
    scoring='accuracy',
    n_jobs=-1
)

train_mean = np.mean(train_scores, axis=1)
val_mean = np.mean(val_scores, axis=1)

plt.plot(param_range, train_mean, 'o-', label='Train')
plt.plot(param_range, val_mean, 'o-', label='Validation')
plt.xlabel('max_depth')
plt.ylabel('Accuracy')
plt.legend()
plt.show()

# === Manual bias-variance decomposition ===
# For a given model and dataset:
# Bias = (train_error + val_error) / 2  (roughly)
# Variance = val_error - train_error (roughly)
# Sweet spot: minimize val_error (not train_error!)
